coredata.dc:description: Pharmaceutical wastewater purification is a paramount challenge, and the production and transportation of H2O2 remain safety concerns. Herein, novel 3-benzoylpropenoic acid was utilized as the electron acceptor linking with carbon nitride through the amide bond, realizing a delighted H2O2 generation of 4.29 mM g-1 cat. h−1, together with a tetracycline removal efficiency of 89 %, which can be attributed to the promoted charge transfer capacity and the strengthened surface adsorption of oxygen. Intriguingly, it is investigated that •O2 – is an important factor for H2O2 synthesis, while •O2 – and •OH both participate in pollutants treatment. Additionally, the applications in various pollutants further manifest the environmental adaptability and extensibility of the system. Additionally, the further introduction of iron ions enables the strengthened activation of H2O2 in a mild condition, thus accelerating the transformation of diclofenac into smaller molecules. Such an investigation paves a promising way for the fabricating carbon nitride based systems with novel electron acceptor, and provide a mechanism guidance for the pollutants treatment paired with H2O2 synthesis.

Simultaneous H2O2 photosynthesis and TC removal: (a) the performance of photocatalysts synthesized with varying amounts of BA and CN, (b) control experiments of CNA10 (PW = pure water, PM = physical mixing of BA and CN), (c) the performance of CNA10 under different pH from 1 to 13, control experiments of CNA10 (PW = pure water, PM = physical mixing of BA and CN), (d-f) the performance of CNA10 in the TC solution with the addition of 10 mM salts or humic acid (10 mg/L) with varying pH conditions, (g) the performance of CNA10 under different monochromatic light radiation; (h) stability test of CNA10 within 20 cycles; (i) the adaptability of CNA10 for some other organic pollutants including antibiotics and dyes (AO7: acid orange 7, RhB: rhodamine B, OTH: oxytetracycline hydrochloride, DCH: doxycycline hyclate; MCH: minocycline hydrochloride, MB: methylene blue, CTH: chlortetracycline hydrochloride, MeTH: methacycline hydrochloride, TH: tetracycline hydrochloride); (j) the versatility of such a synthesized strategy using different acids.

head.ce:title: Investigation into the internal electronic channel and radical contribution in the in-situ H2O2 photosynthesis and concurrent pharmaceutical wastewater cleanup

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Metal-free donor–acceptor-functionalized carbon nitride (CNAx) was synthesized, using 3-benzoylpropionic acid as electron acceptor to realize promoted charge transportation and strengthened oxygen adsorption for H2O2 generation, followed by simultaneous pharmaceutical wastewater treatment. Accordingly, the radical pathway and the contribution of reactive species was investigated thoroughly, and the broad application potential of such a system is emphasized.

Pharmaceutical wastewater purification is a paramount challenge, and the production and transportation of H2O2 remain safety concerns. Herein, novel 3-benzoylpropenoic acid was utilized as the electron acceptor linking with carbon nitride through the amide bond, realizing a delighted H2O2 generation of 4.29 mM g-1 cat. h−1, together with a tetracycline removal efficiency of 89 %, which can be attributed to the promoted charge transfer capacity and the strengthened surface adsorption of oxygen. Intriguingly, it is investigated that •O2 – is an important factor for H2O2 synthesis, while •O2 – and •OH both participate in pollutants treatment. Additionally, the applications in various pollutants further manifest the environmental adaptability and extensibility of the system. Additionally, the further introduction of iron ions enables the strengthened activation of H2O2 in a mild condition, thus accelerating the transformation of diclofenac into smaller molecules. Such an investigation paves a promising way for the fabricating carbon nitride based systems with novel electron acceptor, and provide a mechanism guidance for the pollutants treatment paired with H2O2 synthesis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: The large-scale utilization and persistent existence of pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs) in social development imposes attractive attention on the quality and safety of natural water in recent decades[1,2]. PPCPs, a general term over a class of new contaminants, most of which are synthetic substances, including antibiotics, anti-inflammatory drugs, psychotropic drugs, etc.[3,4]. Of note, PPCPs are exogenous toxic substances for environmental organisms, enriched through the ecological chain, thus seriously threatening human health and the balance of the ecosystem[5]. Tetracycline (TC), as a broad spectrum antibiotic, has been extensively utilized owing to its high antibacterial activity, however, its strong water solubility and chemical stability make it easy accumulated in water, leading to the increase of drug-resistant bacteria or genes and causing harm to humans and animals[6,7]. Diclofenac (DCF), the third generation powerful non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drug with analgesic and anti-inflammatory effects, has been confirmed to be harmful to liver cells and kidney cells of animals and gill cells of fish, thus threatening human health to some extent[8,9]. Inspired by this situation, diverse treatments including membrane filtration adsorption, bio-sorption, and catalytic oxidation have been developed to deal with wastewater containing PPCPs, in which photocatalytic H2O2 photosynthesis and simultaneous wastewater treatment emerged as a promising candidate, owing to its high efficiency and environmental friendliness[10,11]. In this case, the development of efficient catalyst for wastewater treatment with easy preparation is necessary.

Carbon nitride has been considered as one of the effective catalysts for H2O2 production and the degradation of harmful substances, owing to its inexpensive raw materials and suitable band structure[12,13]. Nevertheless, the utilization is still limited owing to its drawback of severe carrier recombination[14]. In light of this, numerous strategies have been developed, including the formation of heterojunctions, the introduction of defects, and the strategies of copolymerization[15–17]. Among them, the introduction of functional groups with electron-withdrawing properties could facilitate directed intramolecular movement of carriers attributed to the π-conjugation effect, effectively accelerating the participation of electrons into the radical pathway of the reaction[18,19]. For instance, Xue et al.[20] found that the introduction of anthraquinone-2-carboxylic acid into CN could achieve efficient electron-hole separation and O2 activation, thereby obtaining high-performance photocatalytic performance. Inspired by this situation, 3-benzoyl acrylic acid (BA) with satisfied π-conjugated structure caught our eyes, which is composed of a double bond and two carbon–oxygen double bonds as well as a benzene ring, and the long chain structure and benzene ring structure might be beneficial for the construction of a donor–acceptor (D-A) structure with efficient transfer of charge carriers[21].

Herein, a donor–acceptor structure was successfully constructed over the combination of 3-benzoyl acrylic acid and carbon nitride through π-conjugation effect, with the formation of an internal electronic channel for promoted charge transportation, and its performance for H2O2 photosynthesis and pharmaceutical wastewater cleanup was investigated. Through EPR and control experiments, it can be analyzed that the main active free radicals involved in H2O2 production is •O2 –, while the main active substances for degradation are •O2 – and •OH, and the proposed degradation pathways as well as mechanism comparison of the simultaneous reaction inside CN and CNA10 are demonstrated. Upon calculation, it was further confirmed that O2 and TC prefer to adsorb on the surface of the composite, with O2 being more likely to adsorb at the electron-rich sites of the BA molecule. Intriguingly, a further activation of H2O2 was realized, achieving efficient removal of diclofenac and implying the practical application capacity of such a system. Undoubtedly, our work offers novel perspectives on utilizing photocatalytic materials for the removal of antibiotics in water purification processes.

Firstly, a crucible containing 20 g melamine was placed into a muffle furnace, and was heated to 500 °C for 2 h and 520 °C for 2 h, with a heating rate of 5 °C /min. Finally, it was cooled down to room temperature and was named as CN.

At 0 °C, 0.78 g of benzene is added dropwise to 20 mL of CH2Cl2 containing 0.98 g of maleic anhydride and 2.66 g of anhydrous AlCl3. After stirring at room temperature for 4 h, it is poured into 20 mL of cold dilute hydrochloric acid. The product is filtered, washed, and recrystallized to obtain yellow solid 3-benzoylpropenoic acid (BA)[22].

0.2 g CN and equivalent of BA were weighed into a round-bottom flask, and then they are mixed evenly with 20 mL acetonitrile, and the final solution is heated at 90 °C for 12 h. The final products were obtained after centrifuging and washing for three times, followed by drying in an oven at 70 °C for 6 h. The final product was named as CNAx (x = 3, 5, 10, 25, 60 correspond to containing 3 %, 5 %, 10 %, 25 %, and 60 % acid respectively).

Following the preparation method mentioned above, benzoic acid, 4-nitrobenzoic acid, 4-chlorobenzoic acid, 4-acetamidobenzoic acid, 4-trifluoromethylbenzoic acid, 2-acetylbenzoic acid, 4-ethylbenzoic acid, 4-methylthiobenzoic acid, cinnamic acid, 4-trifluoromethylcinnamic acid, 4-methoxycinnamate, and salicylic acid are also utilized to replace BA. The final products were named as CNA-1 to CNA-12.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Hydrogen peroxide photosynthesis and organic pollutants removal
ce:section.ce:para: 20 mg catalyst was added into 50 mL pollutants solution with ultrasonication for 20 min, and the solution was stirred in dark for 20 min to allow the system to reach an adsorption–desorption equilibrium (pH = 7). The photocatalytic reactions were carried out under the irradiation of 300 W Xenon lamp after the purging of O2. Every 5 min, 5 mL of solution is taken and filtered through a 0.45 μm microporous filter for measurement

For experiments with varying environmental conditions, some conditions were modified. The investigations with inorganic salts and humic acid interference are conducted upon the aforementioned standard experiment by separately adding 10 mM of HCO3 –, H2PO4 -, Cl-, SO4 2-, NO3 –, or 10 mg/L of humic acid. For experiments under different monochromatic light irradiations, monochromatic filters with wavelengths of 365, 420, 475, and 520 nm were applied. For experiments with different pollutants, they are conducted in a 20 mg/L solution of AO7, RhB, OTH, DCH, MCH, and MB.

The detailed synthesis diagram of CNAx is displayed in Fig. 1
a, in which the –COOH in BA could react with the –NH2 in CN to form CNAx. The crystalline phases of the as-synthesized CN, CNA5, CNA10, CNA25, and CNA60 were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD). As manifested in Fig. 1 b, two diffraction peaks at 12.7° and 27.5° were observed for CN, corresponding to the in-plane repeating pyrazine units of the conjugated aromatic structure and the interlayer stacking, respectively, which can be attributed to the (100) and (002) crystal planes[5]. Besides, no obvious change except for the decreased peak intensity was observed for CNAx, manifesting the stable existence of carbon nitride in the composite and the decreased crystallinity, owing to the fact that the doping with aromatic carbon structures might introduce some defects into the carbon nitride framework and result in weaker crystallinity[23]. Meanwhile, the morphological information is explored through Transmission electron microscopy (TEM). As exhibited in Figs. S3a-S3b, it can be observed that CNA10 exhibits a loose and fragmented morphology when compared with the smooth surface of CN, which is advantageous for further surface adsorption and redox reaction owing to the increased surface area and active sites[24]. Following this, N2 adsorption–desorption isotherms of CN and CNA10 corroborated the increased surface area of CNA10 (Fig. S4), accompanied by the formation of mesoporous and abundant active sites, which is crucial for the engagement of photo-generated charge carriers in surface chemical reactions[25].

The functional groups of CN and CNA10 were performed through Fourier Transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra. The peaks in the regions of 810 cm−1, 1150–1700 cm−1, and 2900–3500 cm−1, were derived from the vibrations of the typical triazine ring units in carbon nitride, the C-N heterocyclic vibrations of aromatic nitrogen with carbon, and the characteristic peaks of –OH and –NH bonds, respectively (Fig. 1 c)[26,27]. Interestingly, it is worth mentioning that new peaks appeared at 720 cm−1 and 2690 cm−1, representing the C–H bending vibrations and the C=O vibrations from the grafting of BA, respectively[20]. This serves as a good proof that BA has been successfully composited with CN. To take a deep understanding of the structure over the as-synthesized CNA10, the solid-state 13C nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectrum was displayed (Fig. 1 d). There exist two typical peaks at 157.5 ppm (d) and 165.4 ppm (c), which might be consistent with the chemical shifts of carbon atoms in “C-N3” and “CN2 (NHx)” within the carbon nitride structure[28,29]. Besides, the typical peaks at 135.5 ppm (e) and 128.5 ppm (f) can be assigned to unsaturated carbon–carbon bonds[30], while peaks a (172.2 ppm) and b (170.3 ppm) correspond to the chemical shifts of carbon atoms double-bonded to oxygen, demonstrating the ideal linking between carbon nitride with BA for the successful synthesis of CNA10 [31].

In this case, the chemical state of each element was further investigated through the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis. As illustrated in Fig. 1 e, there exist three characteristic peaks at 284.8 eV, 286.6 eV, and 288.2 eV for both CN and CNA10, which can be attributed to C–C/C=C, C-NHx, and N-C=N, respectively[24]. The increase of peak intensity over C–C/C=C and C-NHx inside CNA10 might be a result of the incorporation of BA (Table S1)[32]. Additionally, the three components at 398.6 eV, 400.0 eV, and 401.0 eV represent the C=N-C, N-C3, and N-Hx groups within the framework, respectively (Fig. 1 f). It is worth noting that the peaks corresponding to C=N-C (398.6 eV) have shifted to higher fields (398.8 eV) by 0.2 eV, due to the combination of electron-withdrawing groups leading to a decrease in electron density(Table S2)[33,34]. Of note, in addition to the characteristic peak at 532.4 eV for C-O bond in the O 1 s XPS spectrum, two new peaks at 531.4 eV and 533.4 eV can be observed for CNA10 (Fig. 1 g), which can be assigned to the C=O group and –COOH group, further manifesting the successful cooperation of carbon nitride with BA[19,35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: H2O2 photosynthesis paired with simultaneous wastewater treatment
ce:section.ce:para:
Motivated by the compelling synthesis of the photocatalysts, the systematic performances have been carried out to explore the practical feasibility of these materials for the H2O2 formation and concurrent pollutants removal. As shown in Fig. 2
a, it can be ascertained that the addition of BA can lead to the promotion of the redox performance over CN, among which CNA10 exhibited a laudable activity for H2O2 generation (4.29 mM g-1 cat. h−1) and TC removal efficiency of 89 % for the concentration of 20 mg/L TC solution, which is much higher than CN (0.84 mM g-1 cat. h−1 and 28 %) (Fig s . S5-S6). Thereinto, the performances for samples synthesized under varying reaction time and temperature are discussed, in which 12 h and 90 °C were chosen as the optimum reaction conditions (Figs. S7-S8). Thereafter, the control experiments on CNA10 was further carried out (Fig. 2 b), and the addition of TC promoted H2O2 generation when compared to the performance in pure water, signifying the possible existence of redox cycle reaction during the process. On the contrary, the poor performance in the environment without light as well as the decreased activity in Ar and air, disclosing the indispensable roles of light and oxygen in the redox reaction. More importantly, it can be observed that the activity of the physically mixed catalyst was not ideal, validating the necessity of such a synthetic strategy for the modification of CN. As depicted in Fig. S9, we endeavored to assess the stability of CNA10 under extreme temperature conditions, and it was observed that CNA10 demonstrated an maintained performance at an environment of 0 °C, in which the suboptimal kinetics can be ascribed to the low temperature resulted in diminished photocatalytic activity[36,37].

In the case of the environmental adaptability of CNA10, the links between the performance and initial pH/inorganic salt ions have been investigated (Fig. 1 c-f). Intriguingly, it is worth noting that maintained degradation can be observed in a wide pH range of 1–13, implying the practical possibility of the catalyst. However, with the increase of pH, the synthesis of H2O2 was restrained, which might be attributed to the decreased formation of H proton during the H2O2 formation process[38] as well as the fact that H2O2 might be unstable and easy to decompose in this situation[39]. At this juncture, considering the complexity of actual wastewater components, the effects of various anions and salts (HCO3 –, H2PO4 -, Cl-, SO4 2-, NO3 –, humic acid) with varying pH conditions were conducted in the reaction (Fig. 2 d-f). Of note, as exhibited in Fig. 2 d, the addition of H2PO4 -, Cl-, SO4 2-, or NO3 – had a relatively small impact on the reaction in neutral environment. The addition of HCO3 –, H2PO4 -, SO4 2-, and NO3 – might affect the initial pH of solution, and some of those anions compete with the pollutants, consuming •OH or electrons. Nevertheless, both the H2O2 generation and TC removal efficiency were inhibited slightly with the addition of HCO3 –, attributed to the ionization and hydrolysis of HCO3 – might result in an alkaline situation, thus leading to a slight decrease in H2O2 production[40]. Besides, HCO3 – might occupy a part of the active sites, thus affecting the degradation efficiency of TC[41]. The addition of HA might affect the effectiveness of light excitation to generate hole-electron pairs[42], thereby reducing the production of H2O2. Moreover, it might compete with TC during the degradation process, thus affecting the performance of the degradation to some extent[43]. For Cl-, it might influence the degradation efficiency, which can be attributed to the reaction between Cl- and •OH (•OH + Cl- → ClOH•-)[44,45]. For H2PO4 - and SO4 2-, the formation of OH– from the reaction of H2PO4 - or SO4 2- with •OH affects the initial pH and consumes •OH, thereby influencing the generation of H2O2 (•OH + H2PO4 - → OH– + H2PO4•; •OH + SO4 2- → OH– + SO4•-)[46,47]. For NO3 –, the formation of OH– from the reaction of NO3 – with electrons might affect the initial pH of the solution, which affects the production of H2O2 and thus impacts the photocatalytic activity. (2e-+ NO3 – + H2O → e- + NO3 2– + H2O → 2OH– + NO2 –)[47]. Considering the potential interference from heavy metals or surfactants in actual wastewater environments, heavy metal/surfactant tolerance experiments for as-synthesized sample have been conducted (Fig. S10). Although the activity was slightly reduced, it still achieved more than 70 % of its original activity. Sparked by this inspiration, to further verify the stability and anti-interference ability of CNA10 in complex actual water treatment scenarios, the redox capacity of CNA10 under extreme pH conditions was detected (Fig. 2 e-f). Intriguingly, even under such extreme conditions as pH = 1 or 13, the addition of salts showed slight impact on the redox reaction, further implying the anti-interference properties of our synthesized catalyst even under extreme pH conditions[48]. Of note, the influence of HCO3 – seems to be negligible owing to the strong acid environment, which is consistent with the influencing reaction we speculated in the early stage[49]. Besides, for the brevity, although compared to acidic and neutral environments, the production of H2O2 and degradation rate of TC in an alkaline environment (Fig. 2 f) are lower, the addition of inorganic salt ions did not cause significant changes in the photocatalytic results. The series of studies above not only confirmed the environmental adaptability of the catalytic system, but also its superior activity under neutral conditions has paved the way for its practical application.

In this juncture, the performances of CNA10 upon the irradiation of various monochromatic lights were displayed (Fig. 2 g), and the results are highly consistent with the absorption spectrum of CNA10. As shown in Figs. S11-S12, the performance of as-synthesized sample under illumination with different wavelengths and light intensities is considerable, indicating that CNA10 has effective applicability under diverse sunlight conditions. Subsequently, stability test in Fig. 2 h elucidated that a H2O2 production of 3.3 mM g-1 cat. h−1 and a degradation efficiency of 73 % can be maintained even after 20 cycles, unraveling the recyclability of the catalyst for the H2O2 photosynthesis and TC treatment. To further confirm the stability of catalyst during the whole process, the XRD pattern, FTIR spectrum and XPS spectra of CNA10 after reaction are carried out (Figs. S13-S17). To our delight, there are no significant change for CNA10 even after reaction, confirming the possibility of such a system for practical applications. With a focus on the general applicability of CNA10 in practical application, the performances for some other environmental pollutants in daily life, such as antibiotics (OTH, DCH, MCH, CTH, MeTH, TH) and dyes (AO7, RhB, MB), were measured (Fig. 2 i). Fascinatingly, the removal efficiency of most pollutants over CNA10 can exceed 85 % within 30 min, and some even reach 98 %, along with ideal H2O2 production, when compared to poor performance of CN (Fig. S18).

Inspired by this situation, we infer that the promoted activity of CNA10 is the result of the introduction of BA as an electron acceptor and the redistribution of electrons[19]. Thereafter, some other organic small molecule acids (cinnamic acid, benzoic acid, etc.) were utilized to form the similar structure of CNAx for the investigation over H2O2 generation and TC degradation. As displayed in Fig. S19, it can be revealed that the introduction of a conjugated structure might lead to the reduction in crystallinity of the samples[18]. Delightedly, the as-synthesized samples all performed enhanced H2O2 production and TC removal efficiency, demonstrating the universality of this strategy (Fig. 2 j), and the performance of CNA-9 has riveted attention, in which cinnamic acid was utilized as the precursor, with increased specific surface area and abundant active sites (Fig. S20). To take a deeper understanding of the detailed structural change inside CNA-9, some characterizations including FTIR spectra, XPS spectra, and 13C NMR spectrum have been explored (Figs. S21-S23). Similarly, compared with pure carbon nitride, new peaks have appeared near 720 cm−1 (C–H bending vibration) and 1690 cm−1 (C=O vibration) for CNA-9 in FTIR spectra (Fig. S21), as well as the emerging characteristic peaks at 530.7 eV and 534.1 eV in the O 1 s spectrum for O=C group and the –COOH group (Fig. S22), both speculating the successful introduction of cinnamic acid. Furthermore, in the 13C NMR spectrum of CNA-9 (Fig. S23), although there exist new peaks b, e and f, confirming the delighted synthesis of the composite based on carbon nitride and cinnamic acid though the common designed strategy.

The charge transportation capacities of CN and CNA10 were investigated through several photoelectrochemical characterizations. As shown in Fig. 3
a, the enhanced photocurrent curve of CNA10 demonstrated the promoted charge separation efficiency owing to the introduction of BA into the structure for the redistribution of charge density[50]. Combined with the electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) analysis and the linear sweep voltammetry (LSV) curves (Fig. 3 b-c), the smaller semicircle diameter and higher current density of CNA10 corresponds to a lower electron transfer channel resistance and a higher efficiency in charge separation, further indicating the suppressed charge recombination of CNA10 in photocatalytic redox reactions[51]. In addition, the time-resolved photoluminescence (TRPL) analysis is displayed in Fig. 3 d, and it was found that the lifetime decreased from 3.82 ns to 3.62 ns, attributed to the facilitated separation of photo-generated charge carriers and the reduced transportation distance of the charge carriers of CNA10 [52]. Room-temperature photoluminescence (PL) spectra (Fig. S24) collectively confirmed the suppressed photo-generated electrons and holes recombination for CNA10, which is advantageous for the efficient charge transfer for the further H2O2 generation and pollutants removal[53]. Additionally, it is observed that both CN and CNA10 exhibited more negative potentials upon light irradiation (Fig. S25). Apparently, a more pronounced potential difference was exhibited by CNA10, which indicates a more intense electron transfer response inside CNA10 and the introduction of molecule acids connected by amide bonds into carbon nitride might accelerate charge transfer dynamics[54–56].

To take a deep understanding of the numbers of the consumed electrons in the reaction, rotating ring-disk electrodes (RRDE) for CN and CNA10 has been measured (Fig. 3 e). It is acknowledged that the ring current is to the result of the oxidation of the disk, while the disk current originates from the reduction reaction of O2 [57]. Intriguingly, it is indicated that the overall trends of the disk and ring currents for CN and CNA10 are similar, while the slightly greater results demonstrate that more H2O2 can be generated over CNA10 [58]. As displayed in Fig. 3 f, the promoted H2O2 selectivity and decreased number of electron transfer over CNA10 collectively proved that the proportion of the two-electron pathway to produce H2O2 (O2 + 2H+ + 2e- → H2O2) in the reaction is increased, while the four-electron pathway to produce H2O2 (2H2O + 2 h+ → H2O2 + 2H+) is diminished[59,60].

ce:section.ce:section-title: In-deep radical investigation and band structure analysis
ce:section.ce:para:
In order to clearly ascertain the intrinsic mechanism during the reaction, the sacrificial agent capture experiments on the active species over CNA10 and CN were conducted (Fig. 4
a-b), in which AA, TEMP, EDTA-2Na, DDQ, IPA, and CAT were added to quench •O2 –, 1O2, h+, e-, •OH, and H2O2, respectively[61,62]. Conspicuously, •O2 – and 1O2 performed as the pronounced contributors for H2O2 generation, owing to the simultaneous occurrence of the two pathways of ORR (O2 + e- → •O2 – → H2O2; O2 + e- → •O2 – → 1O2 → H2O2)[63,64]. Additionally, the capture of electrons might suppress H2O2 formation during the whole process, thus inhibiting the whole reaction. It is worth noting that the degradation efficiency was reduced from 89 % to 17 % and 40 % after capturing •O2 – and •OH, disclosing the vital roles of •O2 – and •OH in TC degradation. The addition of EDTA-2Na leads to a slight decrease on the performance, indicating the minimal effect of photo-generated holes on pollutants treatment. Furthermore, it can be detected that the removal efficiency was not significantly affected when CAT was added, which can capture H2O2 effectively, speculating that the active species in the reaction process may not be H2O2 [65].

Thereafter, electron paramagnetic resonance (EPR) are utilized to detect the existing reactive species during the redox reaction (Fig. 4 c-e). As exhibited, there was no response signal in the dark, and the signal gradually increased as time progresses, unraveling the generation of reactive species upon light irradiation[66]. As shown in Fig. 4 c, distinct peaks can be observed over CN and CNA10 upon light irradiation, with peak intensities presenting a 1:2:2:1 pattern, confirming the appearance of •OH with the time going[67]. Besides, when 2,2,6,6-tetramethylpiperidine (TEMP) is used as a scavenger, resulting peak ratio of 1:1:1 can be detected, which can be assigned to TEMP-1O2 (Fig. 4 d)[68]. The characteristic peaks of with an intensity ratio of 1:1:1:1 can be investigated under light irradiation (Fig. 4 e), attributed to the formation of •O2 – during the reaction[69]. Furthermore, the stronger signals •OH, 1O2, and •O2 – over CNA10 as compared to CN might be attributed to the n → π* electron transition and strengthened oxygen activation on the surface of CNA10 [70]. It is noteworthy that a lowest kinetic can be observed with the addition of AA, indicating that the vital role of •O2 – in the reaction (Fig. S26). To further investigate the contributions of •O2 – and •OH in both H2O2 synthesis and pollutant removal, quantitative experiments was further carried out (Fig s . S27-S30). By utilizing the stoichiometric parameter that 1 mol of NBT consumption corresponded to 4 mol of •O2 –, the cumulative concentrations of •O2 – in CN and CNA10 were determined to be 0.008 and 0.035 mM, respectively.The quantitative experiment for •OH is shown in Fig. S30, in which benzoic acid can capture •OH to form hydroxybenzoic acid. Therefore, it is indicated that •OH is indeed generated and shows a certain influence on the degradation of pollutants.

Considering the efficient charge separation of CNA10 during the reaction, UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectra (UV–vis DRS) of CN and CNA10 were examined to elucidate the intrinsic structural effects on the photocatalytic activity (Fig. S31). The blue shift in absorption range of CNA10 might be attributed to the quantum confinement effect, resulting from the efficient transportation of charge carriers to active sites following the incorporation of BA into the catalyst[71]. According to the Tauc equation, the calculated bandgap energies (Eg) of CN and CNA10 are 2.49 eV and 2.63 eV, respectively (Fig. S32)[72]. As shown in the valence band X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (VB-XPS) spectra in Fig. S33, the EVBM values of CN and CNA10 are determined to be 1.84 eV and 1.58 eV, respectively. In this case, the calculated EVB values for CN and CNA10 are 1.67 eV and 1.41 eV, respectively, using the formula EVB (NHE) = EVBM + Work function (φ) − 4.44 (φ = 4.27 eV)[32]. In addition, the Mott-Schottky plots of samples are explored in Fig. S34, showing the n-type semiconductor characteristic of the samples, and the flat band potentials (EFB) of CN and CNA10 are −0.92 eV and −1.32 eV, corresponding to the conduction bands of −0.82 eV and −1.22 eV (vs. NHE)[20]. Accordingly, the band structure diagrams of CN and CNA10 are speculated in Fig. 4 f, corroborating the capacity for H2O2 formation and TC degradation in this system[73,74]. This also confirms that the generation process of H2O2 is a two-step single-electron process (O2 + e-→•O2 –, •O2 –+e-+2H+→H2O2), which is consistent with the RRDE results[59].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Surface adsorption and charge distribution calculations
ce:section.ce:para: To elucidate the relationship between the adsorption capacity for O2 and TC over CN and CNA10 structures, computational simulations using density functional theory (DFT) calculations were conducted.

As shown in Fig. 5 , the adsorption energy of CN for O2 is −5.96 eV (Fig. 5 a), while the adsorption energy of CNA10 for O2 is higher at −6.54 eV, −6.48 eV, −6.40 eV, −6.28 eV, −6.17 eV (Fig. 5 b-f). Higher adsorption energy implies that CNA10 has satisfactory superior performance for the generation of H2O2 from O2, attributed to the introduced BA molecules might actively modulate the electron density of the structure[75], which can be demonstrated by the charge difference density (Fig. S35). The charge difference density distribution shows a dense electron density around the BA molecules and nearby oxygen, indicating a higher likelihood of electron transfer activity at these sites. Relatively speaking, the electron density around the carbon nitride framework is sparse, which can well explain the regulated electron distribution density of CNA10 with the addition of BA molecules. This makes CNA10 more likely to engage in the behavior of producing H2O2 with O2, thereby enhancing the photocatalytic reaction activity[58].

Besides, the adsorption capacity of the CN and CNA10 structures for TC is shown in Fig. 6 , in which TC could be adsorbed near the CN structure (Fig. 6 a) and CNA10 has a higher affinity for adsorbing TC, further explaining the promoted activity of CNA10 in TC degradation (Fig. 6 b-f). It is worth noting that TC tends to adsorb preferentially near BA, owing to the interaction with the formed •O2 – in the vicinity that can contribute to the degradation of TC.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Identification of intermediates and products in the degradation
ce:section.ce:para:
The intermediates during the degradation are analyzed using high-performance liquid chromatography-mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS), and the possible reaction pathways are proposed (Fig. 7
, Fig. S36). Initially, as exhibited in pathway Ⅰ, TC molecules can be attacked by •O2 –, undergoing oxygen oxidation to form the larger molecular weight ketone T1 (m/z = 489), and T2 (m/z = 453) is formed with demethylation. Following this, T3 (m/z = 340) is generated through deamidations and dehydroxylation, and it undergoes further oxidation and ring-opening reactions to form T4 (m/z = 223) and further reaction for T5 (m/z = 139)[76]. Secondly, regarding pathway Ⅱ, demethylation of the N-(CH3)2 group on TC leads to the formation of T6 (m/z = 431), and repeated demethylation results in T7 (m/z = 415) and T8 (m/z = 401). Besides, T8 can be further subjected to dehydration and ring-opening reactions under the attack of reactive species (•OH and •O2 –) by these intermediates, resulting in the formation of smaller organic substances T9 (m/z = 135), T10 (m/z = 118), and T11 (m/z = 274)[77]. Finally, pathway Ⅲ undergoes dehydration, with ring being attacked by H+ and dehydrated to form T12 (m/z = 427). Subsequently, the C-N bond is attacked by reactive species such as •O2 – and •OH, leading to the remove of the N-methyl substituent and the deamination, thus forming T13 (m/z = 384). Followed by oxidation, ring-opening, and dehydration reactions, T14 (m/z = 227) is obtained and T15 (m/z = 84) can be further achieved[78]. Ultimately, these organic compounds are mineralized in the system as small molecular compounds such as CO2 and H2O.

Additionally, the activation of H2O2 and the degradation of DCF was carried out over CNA10 with the addition of Fe ions (Fig. S37), with a satisfied performance of 82 % in such a system. Intriguingly, the degradation efficiency of DCF in N2 atmosphere could reach 54 %, indicating that the co-effect of •O2 – and •OH during the process. Following this, the degradation pathway of DCF by CNA10 has also caught our attention (Fig. 8
, Fig. S38). After consideration, the degradation of DCF can be divided into three categories of pathways: halogenation, hydroxylation of the ring structure, and direct cleavage of the bond (carbon–nitrogen)[79]. Firstly, halogenation is caused by the attack of •O2 –, with the Cl atom being the target of its attack to form P1 (m/z = 267). Subsequently, P2 (m/z = 297), P3 (m/z = 314), and P4 (m/z = 303) are obtained through the attacks of •OH and •O2 – [80]. Secondly, through the attack of •OH on the ring structure, P5 (m/z = 162) is formed, and further attacks lead to the formation of P6 (m/z = 128)[81]. Finally, sites with a higher electron cloud density are more prone to electrophilic reactions with •OH to form P7 (m/z = 313), and the cleavage of the carbon–nitrogen bond leads to the formation of P8 (m/z = 163) and P9 (m/z = 152)[82].

Based on the above analysis, the possible mechanism of CNA10 for H2O2 generation and pollutants removal are exhibited (Fig. 9 ). Upon light irradiation, the photo-generated electrons transfer to the electron-attractive BA centers in CNA10, thus suppressing charge recombination and accelerating the surface oxygen reduction reaction with high selectivity of 2e- ORR, in which •O2 – performs as the protagonist for H2O2 formation[83]. On the other hand, the effect of •OH from the H2O2 and H2O for TC removal cannot be ignored, together with the •O2 – generated over oxygen, both dominate the treatment of TC, while holes play a minor role in the direct removal process[84,85].

In summary, the introduction of novel BA molecules as electron acceptor center was realized, which is advantageous for accelerating charge transportation and surface oxygen/pollutants adsorption. Delightedly, a promoted H2O2 generation of 4.29 mM g-1 cat. h−1 can be detected, together with TC removal efficiency of 89 % for 20 mg/L TC solution, which is nearly 5 times and 3 times of CN, respectively. In this case, it has been confirmed that •O2 – is an important factor for H2O2 synthesis, and the major pathway for pollutants treatment includes •O2 – and •OH attacking, during which photo-generated electrons and holes are both necessary for such a synchronous reaction. Intriguingly, such a system could perform satisfied activity with the addition of salts or in varying pH conditions, as well as the long-term stability during 20 cycles with only a slight decrease. Furthermore, its practical application in various pollutants including antibiotics, phenols and dyes further demonstrates the environmental adaptability and extensibility of such a low-cost synthetic process. This research not only provides an in-deep insight into the development of a new type of acids as the electron acceptor center, and give a clearer understanding of the internal mechanism in the concomitant H2O2 generation and pollutants treatment, but also offers a viable solution to the objective reality of drug residue in the aquatic environment.
